title: LevAIthan? A Hobbesian Look at AI | The Aboard Podcast 2026-06-16

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Hi, I'm Paul Ford.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And I'm Rich Ziade.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And this is the Aboard podcast. It's the podcast about how AI is changing the world of software, and boy is it. Today we're going to get philosophical.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: We sure are.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Let's play the theme song and get philosophical.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Rich.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yo.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Do you want to talk about Aboard or do you want to talk about the Mets?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Aboard.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay. You really would rather... Just tell...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I'm not a Mets fan.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, boy you're not.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: But when they lose, it puts me in a good mood. I'm not a Mets hater, even. I don't think I hate the Mets.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I gotta tell you, there's some trick... I don't care much about baseball.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Hate the 7 train.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: First of all, we have to tell everyone that you were a Yankees fan back when they sucked.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I don't need to explain myself anymore.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You do. And no, because honestly, being a Yankees fan is like being a Republican party fan. Like, there's no...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I was too young for the 77-78 Yankees, Reggie Jackson and all the whole lore of that. But I jumped on because everybody loved the Yankees, but the year I jumped on, which is like 1980, they went through a 16-year drought.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, you were there. You were there for 16 years.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I was there. And then one of the most impressive dynasties ever, 96 to 2000, they just decimated everyone. And I've been a Yankee fan ever since.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Then you go to Queens and you got another team.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: The Mets. The Metropolitans.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: The Metropolitans.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They got that baseball head mascot. They're such dorks.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: They're pretty... People love them. People love them. I don't care much about football, but there's a funny thing. Yankees lose, everyone's like 'that sucks, Yankees lose. It's an expensive team.' It's like Citibank losing. They're like 'No, shouldn't happen'.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Mets lose, there's actually infrastructure in the city for the Mets sucking. Like there are... there's probably like a hundred people who make a significant portion of their income just talking about how horrible it is to be a Mets fan.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: That's real. That is real.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's a business, it's part of our culture.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: That's real.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And there's a charm to the Mets. That's the thing. They're kind of... the colors are tacky, they are tacky, they're just corny.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, and when they lose, it's funny. It's very funny because those people come out and overreact.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They do. They do.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Anyway, if you're not in New York City, you're kind of missing one hilarious thing, which is every time the Mets lose when there's like a bad losing streak.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, there's people who are trying to... I mean, they're building personal brands around being angry fans, and it's funny.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's just one of those new media jobs that we don't talk about enough. Anyway, Aboard... Aboard doesn't just talk about the Mets all day. And we know some of you are out there listening because you've been writing in and...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You're hearing what we're doing, but we're going to say it again even so, because it's good for- good for everyone to know what this is all about.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And so, I- I'll try and then I think you could fill it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I'll try.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Thank you, please.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Aboard is a consultancy. You call us up, we have a lot of great software and tools that we've built ourselves, but not only that, we use all the big vibe coding tools, we use all the new wonderful AI-driven software, and the end result is that you come to us with a problem and it comes into view very quickly, like a week or two, and then we figure out how to build from there. So, it's solution engineer—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Engineers, designers, AI therapists.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Mhm. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And we help your org get its head around this new change, but also we just really try to drive value back into your org through software. We're proud of that. I like that. We're doing it for more and more clients to the point that, Rich, what do we need more than anything else in the world?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: People.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: We need solution engineers, yes, which are— it's a new term we like to use, which are product managers that are— like to touch the technology, care about experience and design. Everyone's empowered right now, but product management at its core. We also need engineers, engineers, classic engineers. So, we're hiring, reach out.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Designers too, check the website.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Designers too. Hello at aboard.com or jobs at aboard.com.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So we're growing. We're growing and we're excited.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's been— it took us a minute to get to this point of focus, but boy it's humming.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's humming. So, today we have a very special topic.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oh jeez.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I don't know anything here, anything. You're like 'I got this, I'm going to drive it' and then you turn to me and you said, 'do you know about Thomas Hobbes?' and here's what I know about Thomas Hobbes: very gloomy British philosopher who came up with the idea of like the Leviathan, which I can't even remember exactly what it is.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I'll tell you about Hobbes in a second.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, so please can you educate me?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean I've been thinking a lot— when did you first come across Thomas Hobbes in your life journey as a Lebanese Yankees fan?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I was part of the Brooklyn College core program.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh, let's— let's give it a minute. Let's give it a minute for the— tell people what that is.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It is 10 classes of liberal arts education crafted by Ivy Leaguers actually for CUNY, the City University of New York.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So let's— let's take this back because it's really important, it's very special. You are a knucklehead high schooler.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I got left back in high school.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, you are not— you're not quite sure why you're in high school. You—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I hated authority.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You came over when you're five. Like, it's just a mess. It's a mess.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean you would think I would sort it out after 16 years, you know, 12 years... That's not— that's not how people are.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And so guidance counselor takes you aside, 'Yadi, you're smart. Let's get you off the streets.' It's literally what he said. And then what, like kind of gave you a pen to sign something?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: City of New York... I don't know if they still do this because this is 50 years ago... If they see you're going to...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: graduate, you don't seem to have a plan. They put you into the- into CUNY. CUNY is the City University of New York.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's essentially like, look, no matter what, let's get you off the streets.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And they're like, "Hey, what's your plan, Rich?" And I'm like, "What? I'm gonna have a cheeseburger later," like was my answer or some snarky, obnoxious response.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: They're like, "You going to college?"

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And this is December. Why? Because I got left back, so I'm in the middle of the next year.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Not the greatest grades.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They're like, "You're smart when you go to class." I had like 90s if I liked the teacher and liked the class and I failed miserably, to the point where some teachers gave me like 25s because they wanted revenge because I would be like, "Go to hell," and I'd walk out of the class. It was just a bad scene.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And they're like, "You're going to go to college?"

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And I'm like, "I think so." And I hadn't applied to anywhere.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Wait, in actual- where is this? What neighborhood?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I'm in Canarsie, Brooklyn.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, so this is the- the neighborhood equivalent of getting punched in the face. Like this- you're not- you're not the only kid who's like this.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No, no, no, no, no. I- I hung out at the handball courts. I was that guy. How are we talking about this? I was about to get philosophical.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, I- I gotta- I gotta help people understand how we get from you the- the mess-

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: All right, let me- let me cut through this.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, no, but I- I think it's really important. They literally said, "You're going to college, bud."

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: They said, "You're going to college," like, "If you sign here, you start next week." And then you go- you go to the liberal New Deal university that's almost free with every other kind of human being on Earth, which is Brooklyn College.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Amazing experience.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: One of the great institutions in the world.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: The only reason I did well in college is because nobody cared. And nobody told me what to do. Like, if I was gonna fail in life-

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's on you, buddy.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's on me. And that's all I needed, it turns out. And I took philosophy-

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. 'Cause you were required to.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So they threw you in the swimming pool and you're like, "Okay, I'm swimming."

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I- I- I graduated like 3.8 something out of college. I did very well.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So philosophy, first- I'm assuming this is your first exposure to philosophy.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It is, but the philosophy professor called me into his office and said, "I really would like you to major in philosophy."

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Thank God. You are exhausting as a lawyer. If you had become a graduate in philosophy, I can't even imagine. Just lunch would be a nightmare.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And I said, I said, "What are you talking about, dude?" And I grew up in modest means. Yeah. I was like, "I don't know about any philosophy jobs," so I was like, "I'm not doing that."

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Hey- Hey, dad- your- your dad is like working as a jeweler.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: He's a diamond setter. Yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So I was like, "Cool idea, Professor Taylor." I enjoyed the class, interestingly.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: What a- what a spec- I live very close to Brooklyn College. It's a very special world. The city colleges are wonderful.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Because it's like- it's like kids from the Bronx in a backpack, they're the first like to- to get past high school in their entire history. It's a beautiful thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: First generation City University of New York is a beautiful thing. I think it was $300 a semester. Oh god, it was incredible and it put me on the track and it put me in law school and...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: ...and I won awards for political science because I had- I did real well there. I could have done anything from there, I think, because they put me on a- on a good path.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's a great- great public university. And so they opened your Bay Ridge brain. Literally, and like lots of other students from Bay Ridge.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And what was inside was a pub.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, but let's be clear, you're not in this situation, right? I think people think like, oh scholarship student, like you're kind of the- you were very normal for that college. Like they're taking-

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Everybody's normal.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: They're taking like Brooklyn kids and they're saying you're going to learn a little bit about philosophy.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I didn't understand that most of the students in Brooklyn College were not aspiring to go to Brooklyn College. I thought they let me in to the school.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, you- you thought you'd hit the Harvard of Midwood, right? Yeah, yeah, absolutely.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I did. I didn't know that it was like a place to go so you weren't on the streets.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And- mind you, there are good students who went there, but it was an incredible mix of people. Anyway, in that philosophy class, I learned about Thomas Hobbes. I actually learned about Thomas Hobbes- I was a poly-sci major and I learned about him there as well. So I actually knew- I had read a lot of the political philosophers. I wasn't like- I mean we'd read Descartes and existential philosophy and all that, but it was mostly in political philosophy.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I gotta tell you, I'm a bright enough fellow, we know that. I find this stuff impenetrable. I just get so bored. I'm a fiction guy, I'm a data guy, I can do all- but there's just a certain thing about philosophy, and a little bit with contracts too. Like there's I think your lawyer brain... I just am like boof, it just bounces right off.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's unbearable. No, I think it is for most people, right? It's just kind of horrible.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Um, and I've been thinking a lot about the AI boom.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And Thomas Hobbes comes to mind.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, tell us who Thomas Hobbes is.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Thomas Hobbes was an English philosopher who lived...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay. When are we talking? Kings?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Like the 1600s.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, so Kings in charge, got a little parliament, okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. And what he saw was the English Civil War in the 1600s, early 1600s.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oliver Cromwell, ugly, ugly stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Ugly, ugly stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And, I mean, he observed the mayhem of a civil war. Like-

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: People at each other's throats, chaos, anarchy. A lot of ideas were percolating too around individual liberties and whatnot. And what he found was-

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But stuff is also burning down all the time.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: All the time, right? And because the framework for how people can live together had broken down, he was like, how do we even put this back together?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: religious and cultural things and I-- how like no one can even look each other in the eye and if they do they kill each other.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So- so the civil in civil war. So like our society has fractured over all kinds of-

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and he saw-- he saw the military like really being the only path to order. I'm like how'd we get here? That like now the generals are running everything because we can't behave ourselves. So he saw like mayhem, right? And he wrote a book, very famous book called the Leviathan.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Have you heard of it?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes, that I've heard of it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Alright. So I'm going to read you a quote.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay, the Le-- okay, so Leviathan. What is the Leviathan, by the way?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: The Leviathan is a book-- it's a book on political philosophy. It's just his observation--

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay, so Leviathan, and if I remember correctly, the Leviathan is kind of like the state? Like that's the-- it's the system.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's the state of things, the state that holds power, the people that hold power. Yeah, it's-- it's sort of this entity that is needed to keep order but is not good in a way. And I think he was reacting to, by the way, this is a period where a lot of individual empowerment and liberty was like starting to get espoused in the world and everyone was like, well if you just give everyone individual power, everything will be great. And it turns out it's chaos, right? And so we have to all give up a little, right? And-- and I'm going to actually draw a dotted line back to AI right now.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay, so essentially, hey, wouldn't it be amazing if we all had total freedom and could do what we want? Answer-- Hobbesian answer: no, not really.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's the famous quote, I'll read it to you. It's: life without a common authority is solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay, so the function of the state-- this is a very famous quote, I've heard this many times. Okay, so-- so the function of the state is to make life less brutish, less short, more like make things better.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I mean, it can do that through tyranny.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, but if you let people go wild they will hit each other in the head with a baseball bat to take the other person's banana.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Correct. And I think look, he's in the middle of like, if you look at the arc of history, like one of the most disruptive times in terms of our understanding of people relative to power and relative to the state. So it's a reboot, it's disruption. Like I'm going-- like disruption literally in a Silicon Valley sense. Like the old rules of like someone being anointed by God as your leader and everyone just-- just revere them and that's how we keep order were thrown out effectively, or being thrown out.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oh and just-- just think about your life back then. Like things do not change. There's relative-- there might be a war over here and there's a little news here and there's a plague and a fire, so there-- stuff happens, but in general there's this sense of like got the king in the castle, life goes on, you pray. Yeah, you pray to the king and the God and now Oliver Cromwell's got on a pair of boots and he's just stomping.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: ...up through the countryside.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Now what? Now... Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So, very, very different world than the one that he was expecting.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes. Yes. And it's like, okay, we don't know the next chapter here. Like, this is not good.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay. And again, he lived through that Civil War where everybody was grabbing power, and it was messy, and it was chaos, etc. So I would argue that we are right now in the story arc of AI in the pre-Hobbesian stage. Let me explain.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay. Pre-Hobbesian, meaning the Civil War hasn't happened yet?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No, we're in the middle of it. We're in the middle of the disruption stage. Okay. And what I mean is this: AI has empowered the individual so profoundly, right? And everyone, everyone is feeling really, really... just... they've leveled up everything. So much so that we're each as individuals producing all kinds of things. Alone. Mostly. Pretty much right now. And we are not very collaborative, right? And I'm going to actually... this isn't an observation. We are experiencing this. I have experienced this at a board. I've come to meetings—let's go to the basics here—and every individual in a five-person meeting has their own version of where the product should go, an internal tool we're building, and not only has their own ideas, they even have like their own build on their laptop and they're telling us that they have... it's going to be awesome tomorrow when I show this to everyone else.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: The hilarious thing is, the thing we're building is a collaboration tool.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It is, okay?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, I'm seeing that, but I'm also seeing other things, which is, a potential client comes in with an incredibly detailed AI-generated product requirements document. Maybe it's in the hundreds of pages.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: There's that too. I want to get to that.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay. No, but they come in and then we use AI to understand what's inside of it because it's unreadable because it's AI-generated. So it's essentially data. So like, a lot of things, there's all this weird stuff where everybody's acting like they're deeply empowered, but ultimately everybody's just kind of using these tools to interpret like what the other person is doing and no one's reading things half the time.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And the every—everyone... we are humans. One of the ways we think we can bring order is by impressing other people. Yeah. We love to impress other people. And what we're coming to realize is that we're like... it's... this revolution has numbed us. Everyone thinks that if I walk into the room with this, everyone's going to stop in their tracks. Meanwhile, everyone else came in with their own toy that they've been creating.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I think... so go back to the original idea and let's restate it and bring it to this because I think... I wasn't quite sure where you were going with this, but I get it. So, the pre-Hobbesian era means that...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, and we talk a lot about regulations, rules for AI. And there's—but this is a little different. This is like, wait a minute, everyone has been given this new power and there is no set of rules and guidance for it. And so they're kind of maximizing their own influence, their own ability to show off and impress others and take control and kind of gain a little territory using these tools. And look, most of us live in a society and we're doing it at work and we're saying look, I made a PowerPoint deck in five minutes, aren't I—aren't I a good person, right? And—and maybe the—and the boss is saying, 'Hey, I want you to do more and more of that,' right? And so we're in this state where we are just kind of manufacturing all this content that looks like the right stuff because that's going to make everybody think that we're really smart. And I do—I want to describe something that I see a lot of. And it's terrible because I decide to go in super, super deep on this stuff. And I vibe code all the time and I really want to understand it, I've written about it, I've talked about it, and there is this moment where you—you make something happen and you go, 'Oh, I cracked it,' because it just used to take so long. And what it's meant is that whenever anybody demos me anything they built with AI, I can't be impressed.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You can't be?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I can't. I—it's one of the number one reasons I think everyone should use these technologies just to understand them because what it means is when somebody shows up with something, you go, 'Oh, I kind of see the prompt that you did to do that. You didn't really build a system, you just slapped some words in a box. It's cool output, good for you.' Like if that's good for you, but it doesn't do anything for me.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, that's right. And then it's hard because you're actually—people are really hurt because you're essentially telling them their baby's ugly, even though it was a baby they gave birth to in about six hours instead of nine months.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes. So anyway, that's our—we're in this pre-Hobbesian state.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes, we are. There's a few things that have happened here. One, we're producing too much stuff. We had a prospect come to us and say, 'I have the requirements, I just need you to build it.' She gave us, I kid you not, a dense 80-page requirements document. Clearly she didn't write it. It was so complex and and vast that no one read it. I told her, 'We're not going to read this. It's too much stuff.'

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, let's be clear, she didn't write it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: She didn't write it. She didn't write it. She was very proud of it. She didn't write it. She's not a technologist, out of fairness to her, she's a business stakeholder, but she's like, 'Wow, I just skipped three months. Go build this.' And it wasn't good. We actually sifted through it because it was just generated, right? Number one.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: She doesn't have the necessary—she had the intent to create it, but she didn't have the necessary intent to fully understand it. I think things that are created without full comprehension with AI tend to be not good.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Process is dead right now. There are no speed bumps that force you to slow down a bit and to work together. The coordination that often, it's a little bit of friction, because the expertise that's necessary to slipstream into a project is kind of been thrown by the wayside.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: My slogan: AI punishes laziness.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It does, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Always. Yeah. Problem one: so we're producing stuff, we're not impressing each other, we're probably creating more confusion than anything. Problem two, and this is a big one. And I am all about velocity, let's go, small teams, etc.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Valley hates process, baby. Just hates it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Valley hates process, but complex things require coordination.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You're not, you're preaching to the choir here.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I'm not even saying let's go back to more bureaucracy here. What I'm saying is, here's what has been happening and it's a perfect storm. The engineers are showing up with designs. The designers are annoyed and they're like, I'll show you, and they're using Figma outputs to make code. And they're coming to the meeting with that, right? I'm the customer, I'm the stakeholder. I'm like, what is all this? And everyone is doing everything. And because process is broken down, and I'm going to say something and I've said this to the team, so I'm allowed to say it. I have been asking for release notes and a point release structure, like once a week, call it 0.1, for months. And they summarily ignore me. I don't know if I sound old and boring in this new world, but I'm for, and all I'm trying to do—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, let's, first of all, we've talked about this with the team. This is not, no one's going under the bus here. But wait a minute. Be the empathetic leader, I know that you are. Why? Why is it so hard for people to do that right now?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I know how to drive a race car. Yes. And you want me to get back in a Ford Fiesta? I don't want to do it. They can't believe how productive they feel. But it's an individual productivity that is not additive to a broader goal. Everyone needs to slow down a minute here. Look, I'm not saying this because I'm being high-minded. I'm saying this because there is nothing but shit product being put out right now because everybody is spinning up demos and glomming them together and hoping for the best. We're working on a really amazing product right now and most of the work left is to shave off the nonsense.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well this is, you know I keep, so I'm building a CRM for the company.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You are.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Fun for me. Yes. And I'm doing it totally alone because other people are occupied. Yes. It's got to come into the org.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So I keep showing you where I'm at and you keep going—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's a little much.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I'm like, I absolutely agree. All I'm doing is taking things away from it, right? And it's so hard because what I'd rather do is just build new functions. The only problem is I started using it every day.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And as I started using it every day, it became to confuse me and I'm like, I need so much less product. I need to be able to just chat with this thing, I need to be able to move some cards around.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Now, it does some wonderful things that you can only do with AI. Really proud of that part. It really helps me manage relationships.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Ship's not just data.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's all incredible.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's all incredible. But the larger issue is I probably built, I would say easily ten times too much software. And I probably should have gone for a bunch of bike rides and thought about what I needed and what I wanted.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I might have- I might have shaved a month or two off this process 'cause it's taken a long time.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes. Do you feel like you want people involved to make it better?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Absolutely now.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Part of this was I really-

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Well, you're a special case, by the way, also because you've been working alone.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I also need to understand the technologies if I'm gonna be in the cultural position of explaining them.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So I'm like, I gotta just kind of have my hands really in the clay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I want you to imagine, you ever see the like the one-man band outfit?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh yeah, like the guy's got the cymbals between the knees and all that silliness.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Exactly. Playing the kazoo. This is the equivalent of rather than- oh, let me make the point first and then use this analogy. Expertise has been marginalized. I'm really glad that the engineers, who have no taste- no taste whatsoever-

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Not all of them, but there's- they have their own taste.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They have their own taste- are designing now. The sepia-toned internet that is the product of Claude is among- we are witnessing its glory.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We are really- people are missing something about humans in this situation, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: This is the really- the point of all of this. The expertise has been marginalized. Designers with ten years of user experience experience are really useful.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They are really useful. They understand what to cut out, they understand how to empathize with the user, they understand all those things.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Look, my friend, there are moments where I was looking at this technology- I wrote about this, where you go 'Whoa. I don't know what happens now' because it did things that people used to work on for weeks.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It just actually isn't landing that way. It's landing as like, 'Boy, we need some UX people in on this stat' because everything looks kind of the same.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Everything looks the same. It's not even just looks, by the way. There's bad usability everywhere right now.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, it's not controlled, right? Like, let's just actually take a second for the listener, because I think we should compare 'Hey Claude, will you make me something?' Okay, versus 'We took the brand and we synthesized it into a really nice branding guide that's online. You can cut and paste the components to build compliant, attractive components for all of our users. And it's accessible and it works in right-to-left and left-to-right languages.' Now, I can use AI to do all of that. But thinking about it holistically and owning that part of the product as a sub-product unto itself, advocating for it, caring about how it actually walks over to the user and how they use it, is a discipline unto itself. AI, for whatever reason, cannot kill that. And everybody was worried it could, but it just can't. At least so far. So it's this very funny moment.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Because everybody's super empowered. Yeah. But we're actually and I'll tell you what you and I are trying to do as an org, we have let everybody have lots of tokens. They're doing all kinds of stuff. And now we are slowly pulling them back to the actual disciplines that they know best.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Absolutely necessary.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's really necessary. And so like if you're a product manager out there, it's great that we have you here. It's great that you know how to use this stuff. We need your product management brain more than we need you to just be really good slamming words into the Claude box.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: This is coming. And no, but we're also like, this is what our clients want us to do. Like we're getting this demand to solve these problems.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: People are not working together right now.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's what we got to fix. Right. So, so that's, you need, that is not us, by the way.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No. That is happening. That is happening in the world.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We just don't lie about it. We don't lie about it. And we're experiencing it and we're going to work through it and we're going to tilt our culture to towards towards a different place. This is another element too, which is I'm just like patient zero here. Like I started messing around with it. I was like, whoa. Yeah, yeah, yeah. I've been through all of it and I kind of have a lot of empathy for where people are landing.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: All right. So Rich. Yeah. Hobbes. Do we we're pre-Hobbesian, not good. Everybody's running in a bunch of different directions.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Now Hobbes gets here. It's all good. What does that even mean?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Sort of.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Hobbes advocated for the monarchy as sort of the source of order.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Do you ever think though that maybe it would be better if we just had a king and just called it a day here in democracy?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Well, I got news for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Like I just, you know what I think about, I think about the British like having the that it's almost like an interface for people who cannot understand anything more complicated than a king. They go, we got one. It's okay. Don't you worry about a thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They do.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. And then over here we got the prime minister and it's like they're they're always really boring.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Very boring. The cabinet.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. But if you if that is too boring for you, you look over here and you have some of the the goofiest people on earth.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Wearing crowns. Yeah. Yeah. Exactly.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Anyway, it's an interface to culture. All right. Wait, are you pro-monarchy now?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I am pro, oh boy, as a caveat. I am pro better outcomes and pro people working together. And the reason I say that is if you look at what gets flashed around right now, it's small things. It's demos, it's videos, usually eight seconds long. It's images, it's it's stuff that you can do on your own to make a point.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, no. I'm building a large new platform. A large new platform is not radically simpler.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, exactly.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Building core pieces of it is radically simpler, but assembling it into one coherent product is kind of as hard as it ever used to be.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: A lot of this is about software, but I if you think about I'm going to make a movie using AI, there are people who are great at soundtracks, great at sound effects, great at visuals.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Visual effects, great at lighting, there are cinematographers that think about how light should be cast, there's all these skills that are out there. We have decided that I have all the skills and I don't need anyone else. And what's happened is these one-man bands are all showing up to the orchestra pit and it sounds like shit.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Mhm.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And the truth is there is an orchestration and a coordination that is going to lead us to another place with all this stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I'm reading a book about a guy who's trying to teach himself Bach, okay, on the piano. Okay? And I'm doing that too.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And you know what your teacher will tell you to do is, 'Hey, you gotta record yourself so you can hear it'.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Must be bad.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And you record yourself, and it sounded pretty good.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Uh-huh.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Then you play it back and you're like a dog threw up on the keyboard and then jumped on the throw up. Like, it's so bad. And I really think that's what's happening when people generate things. Like, they're like, 'Here it is, look, I am the director'.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I'm gonna give myself a gold star. This is pretty darn good.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's so gratifying.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes. And then you send it out to be perceived and people are like, 'I hate it'. And then everybody gets hostile about that. They're like, 'Wait, what do you mean you hate it? It looks just like a movie.' And they're like, 'It doesn't.' 'Why not? It's got really good lighting.' Bar-bar-bar. So what you just described, right? And I think you've got the pre-Hobbesian brain going like, 'I made it, I'm Spielberg'.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We've got to relinquish some control, as per Hobbes. We can't have absolute freedom. We need some humility. We still need to respect other people's expertise. That still matters. So I have opinions about branding and colors, but there are people who really think that stuff through and they're really good at it and I'd rather they present back to me. I know I can whip together a logo. I can whip together a hundred logos. It's not the point, right? And that is going to still matter because we're hitting a wall. We're hitting a wall of slop. And to go beyond it and to scale up with all this amazing stuff, we actually have to work together.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You know my thesis, which is increasingly that LLMs—they're really good at parsing and making sense of natural language, but that most of what they're doing as far as I'm concerned is getting me access to more of the power inside of the computer. So I can use a database more effectively and more broadly. I can use machine learning models, I can do things with statistics and visualization, which are essentially old computer things using the new computer thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You do that a lot, I've noticed.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And it's—that loop is very virtuous because you end up getting more and more stable as time goes on. I did teach a computer to make vaporwave songs.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's great. Let's go back to Hobbes.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: We're done. That's the message here.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, so—so we need a monarchy.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: What do we need? Come on, man. Okay, tell—tell us what to advocate for. You just told us we can't make cool movies.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: We need to respect each other's capabilities. We need a pinch of process. We need some speed bumps so that we can check in on each other. We need to lean on each other's skills and experience so we can have better, bigger outcomes. Right now we have these cute little outcomes that, yeah, you are productive at your desk. Don't pick up the colorful thing you made at your desk and walk it into a meeting. It's not helping.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Let me tell you something that everybody knows in the world of the creative arts. It was a great quote. It was a socialist and builder named William Morris, a legendary person in the late-

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Here comes the name dropping from the other guy. This is my show!

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Late 1800s UK, and he was- he was writing to a friend, and he was actually complaining about typewriters. He's like, 'this isn't- you gotta use a quill pen.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Sure, sure, sure. I'm sounding a little old school.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, no, he was very old school. And he had a great line, and the line was 'you can't have art without resistance in the material.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: There it is.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And you cannot have creativity without constraints. And it's not just- it's not just that the constraints can give you a to-do list. The form and the structure of things are how people understand what you're doing. If I suddenly start singing a song right now, that would seem bananas. But if I said, 'Rich, I've been composing funny little songs. I want you to hear a couple of them,' you'd be like, 'All right, I'll evaluate based on what I think a song should sound like.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Software was so expensive that we had zillions of processes to manage the costs because failure was a normal and natural constraint, and we lived inside of that. And now people no longer feel that constraint. They don't feel it in making videos and images and prose. But if- if the constraints aren't there, no one can perceive what you're doing. It's just more slop. That's what slop is. Slop is a slip out of the form that somebody's expecting. You're like, 'I thought that would be a nice picture of a girl, but her face is all kind of like- it's like a little glowy. I don't know, it feels weird.' And so I see it with the hobbyist-ism. I think you don't even need the monarch. You just need people to start to assert the value of the form. And one of the forms that has value is good, simple software that doesn't have eight million features, but is actually focused on a particular group of people achieving a shared goal.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes. And I think that right now for all the power of these tools, they are not optimized for building that. We've talked in the past about how enterprises are having a hard time with this stuff, not a lot of success stories, and I'm pretty convinced it's because you just don't glom together individual contributions into one giant thing. It doesn't work.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, it used to be that the different forms and the different disciplines would come together and be like-

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They would.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, we only get this many story points. We have to do this meeting every-

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And we're going to get designs, and then engineering will take the designs. I'm not saying go back to that. It's a new world, but-

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, but literally, that was the world. If you think about like agile methodologies, they're almost economic methods.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: That's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That not being necessary has what you'd think would open this great flowering and unfolding.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's not.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But it's just a lot of mess, and what it means is that people on the other side can't get their artifacts and get their work done. Correct?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So, okay. All right, so it's time to get out of that Hobbesian state.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes. And build a little, build a little monarch in your heart.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: That's cute.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Today's podcast was brought to you by Thomas Hobbes and Brooklyn College and René Descartes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh, yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Reach out. We love to talk to companies, organizations, nonprofits. At Aboard, we slow things down just a bit. We leverage all these tools and we put great people on it. They work together to ship amazing solutions for our clients.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Turns out that's what our clients want.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. They're like, 'Okay, good, you're taking this step by step. I feel a lot better about that.' Yeah. All right, so that's what we do. We like doing it. I like helping people. I like building with these new tools. I do love the acceleration. It is fun.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It is fun, but I mean you build too much software and it doesn't do anybody any good.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Doesn't do anybody any good.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, it's like, you know what? You know what's not good? A 5,000-page book. Right? You need to... let's get editing in there.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Except for The Power Broker by Robert Caro.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You hear that's only like 1600. I couldn't get through. He was like in swim class in page 70 and I was like, 'I can't do this.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's just, yeah. I'm like, 'I'll just, I'll just go to the beach instead.'

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: hello@aboard.com. Give us five stars, thumbs up, whatever ways you can express your positive impression.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Go to the website if you want to learn about us and if you want to work here. aboard.com. Apply to work here. We'd love to talk to you.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, have a great week. Bye.

highlights: The current AI landscape is described as "pre-Hobbesian," a period of disruption where extreme individual empowerment has led to a breakdown in organizational coordination and a surplus of unvetted content.
AI often "punishes laziness" by allowing users to generate massive amounts of output—such as 80-page requirements documents—that lack true human comprehension and ultimately fail to provide value.

The democratization of tools has led to the marginalization of expertise, where individuals use AI to cross into disciplines like design or engineering without the deep experience required for high-quality usability.
Without the traditional constraints of cost and technical difficulty, software development risks becoming "slop," echoing the sentiment that "you can't have art without resistance in the material.".

To build coherent and valuable products, organizations must move away from isolated individual productivity and return to a "pinch of process" that respects specialized disciplines and collective orchestration.
summary:
In this episode of the *Aboard* podcast, hosts Paul Ford and Rich Ziade explore the current state of the AI revolution through the lens of 17th-century political philosophy. After a brief discussion regarding the cultural nuances of New York baseball and the growth of their software consultancy, Ziade introduces Thomas Hobbes’s *Leviathan* as a metaphor for the modern technological landscape. He argues that the tech world is currently in a "pre-Hobbesian" state of disruption—a chaotic era where the radical empowerment of individuals has led to a breakdown in collaboration and order, mirroring the social anarchy Hobbes observed during the English Civil War.

The central theme of the discussion is that while AI tools allow individuals to "vibe code" and generate massive amounts of content instantly, this "super-empowerment" often results in poor quality and "slop." Ziade and Ford observe that because traditional processes and specialized expertise are being marginalized, professional environments are becoming cluttered with uncoordinated individual demos and AI-generated documents that no one actually reads. They argue that AI often "punishes laziness," as the ease of production frequently bypasses the critical thinking and human empathy required to build a cohesive, usable product.

Ultimately, the hosts conclude that the industry must move beyond this chaotic phase by reintroducing a "pinch of process" and respecting distinct professional disciplines like UX design and product management. They highlight that while AI can accelerate individual tasks, the difficult work of orchestration and editing remains a uniquely human necessity. By advocating for humility and the value of shared goals, Ford and Ziade suggest that true innovation requires the "resistance" of constraints and a return to collaborative expertise to turn raw AI output into meaningful software solutions.
description:
Is the AI era destined to make our software lives nasty, brutish, and short? On this week’s podcast, Rich brings Paul the framing of “Hobbesian AI,” specifically from 17th century English philosopher Thomas Hobbes’s greatest work, Leviathan. Humans need some kind of order, Hobbes argued in the wake of the English Civil War, otherwise they will destroy each other. How can these lessons be applied to our decidedly order-less AI moment? 

Captions are auto-generated; for audio, show notes, and a full transcript, visit our website: https://aboard.com/podcast/levaithan-a-hobbesian-look-at-ai/
topics: Applying Thomas Hobbes’s 'Leviathan' and political philosophy to the current AI disruption
The tension between individual AI productivity and the need for collaborative software development
The ongoing necessity of professional expertise in design, engineering, and product management
Aboard’s consultancy model and its hiring focus on 'solution engineers'
The concept of 'AI slop' and why creative constraints are essential for quality software
